details.text: - “Who (الَّذِي) created (خَلَقَ)”: This is an absolute and restricted statement where the object of creation is not mentioned implying that He created everything and there is no limit to His creation.

- There are different interpretations for the meaning of اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ. It has been said that the بِ in بِاسْمِ is added (زَائِدَة) but is omitted in understanding so that اسْمِ رَبِّكَ becomes the object of the verb (المَفْعول بِهِ – Al-Maf’ul Bihi) and would mean “Read the name of your Lord”. Another interpretation is that بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ refers to “what was revealed (to you) from your Lord” and the statement would therefore mean: “Read what was revealed to you from your Rabb (Lord and Master)”. بِ can also mean “with” implying support and help and the statement would then mean: “Read with the help of your Rabb (Lord)”. بِ can also imply “in” making the meaning: “Read in the name of your Lord”. It has been stated that by “Reading the name of Allah” or “Reading in the name of Allah Ar-Rahman Ar-Rahim” you are making clear that what you are reciting is directly from Allah and nobody else. As such, the Messenger ﷺ is being informed that he should be aware that when he reads, he is reading from His Lord, and when he conveys it, he is doing so in the name of his Rabb (Lord).
Derivative: رَأَيْتَ “You seen” is from رَأَى – رَأْيٌ which means to physically see but also means to consider, formulate an opinion or discern something.

Derivative: النَّاصِيَةِ refers to the forelock i.e., the hair at the front part of the head. Dragging somebody by النَّاصِيَةِ (the forelock) was a way the Arabs used to humiliate and punish a person.

- لَنَسْفَعًا – This has لام التَّوْكِيد (Laam At-Tawkeed) prefixed to the verb and نُون التَّوْكِيد (Nun At-Tawkeed) affixed to it. Both are for the purpose of emphasis. The meaning is therefore “We will certainly and definitely grab or drag him by the forelock”.
- لَئِن is made up of لَ and إِنْ and the لَ is لام التَّوْكِيد (Laam At-Tawkeed) or لَام قَسْم (Laam Qasm - Laam of an oath) which is used here to emphasise a warning or threat.
Derivative: خَاطِئَةٍ is from خَطَأ which means to commit a mistake, offense or sinful act. It has been said to refer to the type of mistake the consequences of which are not known when perpetrated.

Derivative: زَبَانِيَةَ is the plural of زَبْنِيَة and refers to the police or prison guards in the case where the verb زَبَنَ means to direct and push people with force, severity and harshness. Here it is referring to the guards of hellfire.

Derivative: عَلَقٍ (‘Alaq) comes from the verb عَلِقَ (‘Aliqa) which means to cling, catch (on to something) and to hang or dangle. عَلَقٍ (‘Alaq) literally means something that hangs off of something and refers to a clinging form.
Derivative: الْأَكْرَمُ is the superlative form of كَرَم which refers to something that should be respected in and of itself and means the Most Noble.
Derivative: كَلَّا can mean أَلا (Alaa) which means “Unquestionably” or حَقَّا (Haqqan) which means “For sure” or “Truly”. It can also imply a scolding and rebuke directed towards the rebellious.
Derivative: يَطْغَىٰ means to rebel and transgress against the limits whilst being cognizant of those limits. It refers to excessive rebellion.
Derivative: اسْتَغْنَىٰ means free of need and self-sufficient. In respect to the human, it is a sign of the highest level of arrogance.
- رَبِّكَ “Your Lord” – This reflects a transition from the third person to the second person in the case where Allah is addressing الإِنْسان (the human) directly here.
